# Pedi-Growth: AI-Powered Pediatric Gait Analysis and Screening System {#pedi-growth:-ai-powered-pediatric-gait-analysis-and-screening-system}

**Id** : 2331178

**Course Title**: Web Applications & Internet

**Department of Computer Science and Engineering**

**Independent University, Bangladesh (IUB)**

Submission Date: 4 June

Supervisor: Md Abu Sayed

# 

# 

# **1\. Introduction** {#1.-introduction}

## **1.1 Purpose** {#1.1-purpose}

This Software Requirements Specification (SRS) describes the main requirements of Pedi-Growth, an AI-based pediatric gait analysis and screening system. It is intended to guide the project team and other stakeholders during design, development, testing, and future maintenance. It explains what the system is expected to do, how users and system components will interact with it, and which technical and clinical limitations must be considered. Pedi-Growth uses smartphone videos, computer vision, and basic biomechanical measurements to screen a child's walking pattern for possible abnormalities. The system supports clinical decision-making, but it does not replace an examination or diagnosis by a qualified healthcare professional. The document follows the general structure of IEEE 830 and ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 so that the requirements remain clear, organized, and traceable.

## **1.2 Scope** {#1.2-scope}

Pedi-Growth is a web-based platform for analyzing the gait of children from birth to 18 years of age. It uses markerless pose estimation on videos recorded with a normal smartphone or digital camera, which makes the process more affordable than a laboratory-based motion-capture setup. The current scope includes video upload, pose estimation, gait-metric calculation, abnormality screening, and report generation. The main objectives are to:

* Provide an affordable and accessible platform for basic gait screening.  
* Support physicians and health workers during pediatric gait assessment.  
* Use AI and computer vision to automate the extraction of biomechanical measurements.  
* Protect patient privacy through secure processing, controlled access, and facial anonymization where applicable.  
* Present results through standardized reports and visual summaries that can support evidence-based clinical decisions.

## **1.3 Definitions, Acronyms and Abbreviations** {#1.3-definitions,-acronyms-and-abbreviations}

| Term / Acronym | Definition |
| ----- | ----- |
| **AI** | Artificial Intelligence; computer-based methods that perform tasks such as pattern recognition, prediction, and decision support. |
| **API** | Application Programming Interface; a defined way for software components to exchange requests and data. |
| **BMI** | Body Mass Index; a value calculated from a person's height and weight and commonly used as a general body-composition indicator. |
| **CHW** | Community Health Worker; a trained worker who provides basic healthcare and supports patients, especially in rural or underserved areas. |
| **Computer Vision** | A field of AI that enables computers to interpret and process information from images and videos. |
| **FastAPI** | A modern Python-based web framework used to develop high-performance backend APIs for the Pedi-Growth system. |
| **Frontend** | The client-side interface through which users interact with the system using a web browser. |
| **Backend** | The server-side application responsible for business logic, data processing, AI inference, authentication, and communication with the database. |
| **Gait Analysis** | The systematic study of human walking patterns to identify biomechanical characteristics and potential movement abnormalities. |
| **Gait Cycle** | A complete sequence of walking movements beginning when one foot contacts the ground and ending when the same foot contacts the ground again. |
| **GPSI** | Gait Pattern Severity Index; an overall severity score generated by the system to summarize the likelihood and intensity of gait abnormalities. |
| **HIPAA** | Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act; a United States regulation establishing standards for protecting sensitive patient health information. |
| **JSON** | JavaScript Object Notation; a lightweight data-interchange format commonly used for API communication. |
| **JWT** | JSON Web Token; a secure token-based authentication mechanism used for verifying user identity during API requests. |
| **MediaPipe** | Google's machine learning framework for real-time human pose estimation, used to extract skeletal landmarks from uploaded gait videos. |
| **Next.js** | A React-based web framework used to develop the responsive frontend interface of Pedi-Growth. |
| **OpenCV** | Open Source Computer Vision Library; a software library used for image and video processing tasks. |
| **OpenRouter AI** | An AI service used to generate clinician-friendly summaries and interpretations of gait analysis results based on extracted biomechanical metrics. |
| **PDF** | Portable Document Format; the standard document format used for exporting gait analysis reports. |
| **Pose Estimation** | The process of identifying and tracking anatomical body landmarks from images or videos without the need for physical markers. |
| **PostgreSQL** | An open-source relational database management system used to store user information, patient records, analysis history, and system metadata. |
| **REST API** | Representational State Transfer Application Programming Interface; a web service architecture used for communication between the frontend and backend components. |
| **SRS** | Software Requirements Specification; a formal document describing the functional and non-functional requirements of a software system. |
| **Supabase** | A Backend-as-a-Service platform providing PostgreSQL database services, authentication, file storage, and backend infrastructure for the system. |
| **UI** | User Interface; the graphical environment through which users interact with the application. |
| **UML** | Unified Modeling Language; a standardized modeling language used to visualize and document software system architecture and behavior. |
| **Video Processing Pipeline** | The sequence of computational stages responsible for processing uploaded gait videos, extracting skeletal landmarks, calculating biomechanical metrics, and generating clinical reports. |
| **Web Application** | A software application accessed through a web browser without requiring local installation on the user's device. |

## **1.4 References** {#1.4-references}

| Reference ID | Description |
| ----- | ----- |
| **\[R1\]** | ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018. *Systems and Software Engineering — Life Cycle Processes — Requirements Engineering.* IEEE Standards Association. |
| **\[R2\]** | IEEE Std 830-1998. *IEEE Recommended Practice for Software Requirements Specifications.* IEEE Computer Society. |
| **\[R3\]** | Google. *MediaPipe Documentation.* [https://developers.google.com/mediapipe](https://developers.google.com/mediapipe) |
| **\[R4\]** | OpenCV Team. *OpenCV Documentation.* [https://docs.opencv.org](https://docs.opencv.org/) |
| **\[R5\]** | FastAPI Documentation. [https://fastapi.tiangolo.com](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/) |
| **\[R6\]** | Next.js Documentation. [https://nextjs.org/docs](https://nextjs.org/docs) |
| **\[R7\]** | Supabase Documentation. [https://supabase.com/docs](https://supabase.com/docs) |
| **\[R8\]** | PostgreSQL Global Development Group. *PostgreSQL Documentation.* [https://www.postgresql.org/docs](https://www.postgresql.org/docs) |
| **\[R9\]** | OpenRouter Documentation. [https://openrouter.ai/docs](https://openrouter.ai/docs) |
| **\[R10\]** | World Health Organization (WHO). Resources related to child growth monitoring and developmental assessment. |
| **\[R11\]** | Selected peer-reviewed literature on pediatric gait analysis, markerless motion capture, biomechanics, artificial intelligence, and computer vision used during system research and requirement elicitation. |

These references were used to guide the software structure, selected technologies, and clinical screening approach of Pedi-Growth.

## **1.5 Overview** {#1.5-overview}

This SRS is divided into four main chapters so that the project requirements can be followed more easily during implementation and testing.

Chapter 1 introduces the project, its scope, important terms, and the references used.

Chapter 2, "Overall Description," explains the system perspective, major functions, user groups, assumptions, dependencies, and planned future features.

Chapter 3 lists the functional and non-functional requirements, external interfaces, performance targets, and design constraints. Chapter 4 contains supporting information such as requirement elicitation, stakeholder analysis, risks, future scope, and the glossary.

Together, these chapters provide a common reference for developers, testers, researchers, the project supervisor, and other stakeholders involved in building and evaluating the system.

# **2\. Overall Description** {#2.-overall-description}

Pedi-Growth extracts gait measurements from smartphone videos and uses them to screen for possible movement abnormalities. Because it does not require body markers or a specialized gait laboratory, it can be used in clinics as well as in community or remote settings. Its modular architecture separates video processing, AI analysis, and data management so that each part can be developed and maintained independently.

## **2.1 Product Perspective** {#2.1-product-perspective}

The system uses computer vision to identify body landmarks in each video frame and then calculates gait-related measurements from those landmarks. The main architecture includes a Next.js frontend, a FastAPI backend, MediaPipe and OpenCV for video analysis, and OpenRouter AI for generating readable summaries. Supabase and PostgreSQL are used for authentication, storage, and controlled access to patient records.

**![][image1]**

Figure 2.1 System Architecture

### **2.1.1 System Interfaces** {#2.1.1-system-interfaces}

Pedi-Growth is built as a set of connected web modules. The frontend, backend, AI-processing components, and cloud services communicate through REST APIs. This layered setup keeps the responsibilities of each module separate and makes the system easier to secure, test, and expand.

The main system interfaces are summarized below.

| Interface | Purpose |
| ----- | ----- |
| Frontend ↔ Backend | Facilitates communication between the Next.js user interface and the FastAPI backend through RESTful APIs. |
| Backend ↔ Authentication Service | Authenticates users, manages login sessions, and enforces role-based access control using Supabase Authentication. |
| Backend ↔ Database | Stores and retrieves patient records, gait analyses, generated reports, and user information from the PostgreSQL database. |
| Backend ↔ File Storage | Uploads and retrieves gait videos, processed files, and downloadable PDF reports using Supabase Storage. |
| Backend ↔ AI Processing Engine | Sends uploaded gait videos for pose estimation, biomechanical analysis, and gait metric computation. |
| AI Processing Engine ↔ OpenRouter AI | Generates clinician-friendly summaries and interpretations based on calculated gait metrics and analysis results. |
| Backend ↔ PDF Report Generator | Produces structured gait analysis reports for download and future reference. |

Together, these interfaces allow data to move between modules without tightly coupling the whole system.

### **2.1.2 User Interfaces** {#2.1.2-user-interfaces}

The web interface is designed to be simple enough for users who may not have technical knowledge. The main workflow should be clear from patient selection to video upload, analysis, and report viewing.

Pages use a consistent layout, readable labels, and responsive design so that the system can be used from common desktop and mobile browsers.

The main user interface components are:

* Login and Authentication Screen  
* User Registration Interface  
* Dashboard  
* Patient Management Module  
* Video Upload Interface  
* Video Preview Screen  
* Gait Analysis Dashboard  
* Clinical Summary Panel  
* Historical Analysis Records  
* PDF Report Viewer  
* User Profile Management  
* Administrative Dashboard

The interface gives users clear feedback through upload indicators, analysis progress bars, confirmation messages, and understandable error messages.

The system is mainly optimized for desktop and tablet use, while still supporting mobile browsers for community health workers and field users.

| ![][image2] | ![][image3] | ![][image4] |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **![][image5]** | **![][image6]** | **![][image7]** |

Figure 2.2 User Interface Navigation Flow

### **2.1.3 Hardware Interfaces** {#2.1.3-hardware-interfaces}

Pedi-Growth is intended to run on commonly available devices and does not depend on laboratory-grade motion-capture equipment.

The minimum recommended hardware is listed below.

| Component | Minimum Requirement |
| ----- | ----- |
| Client Device | Desktop computer, laptop, tablet, or smartphone capable of running a modern web browser |
| Processor | Dual-Core Processor (Intel Core i3 or equivalent) |
| Memory | 4 GB RAM or higher |
| Storage | Minimum 500 MB of available local storage for temporary file handling |
| Camera | Smartphone or digital camera capable of recording HD video (720p or higher) |
| Display | Minimum screen resolution of 1366 × 768 pixels |
| Internet Connection | Stable broadband or mobile internet connection |
| Server Infrastructure | Cloud-hosted backend with sufficient processing resources for concurrent AI analysis |

Markerless video analysis removes the need for infrared cameras, reflective markers, and force plates in the basic screening workflow.

### 2.1.4 Software Interfaces

The system combines web frameworks, AI libraries, and cloud services to handle the user interface, authentication, video processing, and data storage.

The main software components are listed below.

| Software Component | Purpose |
| ----- | ----- |
| Next.js | Frontend development framework |
| React | User interface rendering and component management |
| FastAPI | Backend REST API development |
| Python | AI processing, backend logic, and machine learning implementation |
| OpenCV | Video preprocessing and computer vision operations |
| MediaPipe | Human pose estimation and landmark extraction |
| NumPy | Numerical computation for biomechanical calculations |
| SciPy | Mathematical and signal processing utilities |
| OpenRouter AI | AI-generated clinical interpretation and summary generation |
| Supabase Authentication | User authentication and authorization |
| PostgreSQL | Relational database management |
| Supabase Storage | Cloud storage for uploaded videos and reports |
| PDF Generation Library | Creation of downloadable gait analysis reports |

These components communicate through secured APIs and use agreed data formats so that information remains consistent across the system.

### 2.1.5 Communication Interfaces {#2.1.5-communication-interfaces}

Standard secure web protocols are used for communication between users, the backend, cloud services, and external AI services.

All REST API traffic carrying patient or account data uses HTTPS with TLS encryption.

The backend connects to authentication, database, storage, and AI modules through protected API endpoints. Normal operations follow a request-response model, while longer tasks such as video analysis and summary generation may run asynchronously so that the interface does not become unresponsive.

The communication technologies used by Pedi-Growth are shown below.

| Communication Interface | Purpose |
| ----- | ----- |
| HTTPS | Secure communication between web browsers and backend services |
| RESTful APIs | Data exchange between frontend, backend, and AI processing modules |
| TLS Encryption | Protects sensitive patient and authentication data during transmission |
| Supabase APIs | Authentication, database access, and cloud storage communication |
| OpenRouter API | AI-assisted generation of clinical summaries and interpretations |

The communication layer validates incoming data and uses suitable retry or recovery methods when a temporary failure occurs.

### 2.1.6 Memory Constraints {#2.1.6-memory-constraints}

Most heavy processing is performed on the server, which allows the client application to run on ordinary computers, tablets, and smartphones.

Uploaded videos and extracted landmark data may be stored temporarily during analysis. Only the records required for patient history, reports, and auditing are kept for the longer term.

Temporary processing files should be removed after analysis when they are no longer needed, helping to control storage usage and protect patient data.

The main storage and memory considerations are:

* Temporary storage of uploaded gait videos during processing.  
* Temporary storage of extracted skeletal landmark data.  
* Efficient memory allocation during pose estimation and biomechanical computation.  
* Cloud-based persistence of finalized patient records and generated reports.  
* Automatic removal of unnecessary temporary processing files after successful analysis.

These measures help the application remain responsive and avoid unnecessary storage use on both the client and server sides.

### 2.1.7 Operations {#2.1.7-operations}

The workflow automates the technical parts of gait processing while keeping the clinician responsible for reviewing and interpreting the final result.

A typical analysis follows these steps:

1. The user authenticates using secure login credentials.  
2. A new patient profile is created or an existing patient record is selected.  
3. A gait video is uploaded through the web interface.  
4. The backend validates the uploaded video for compatibility and quality.  
5. The AI processing pipeline extracts skeletal landmarks using MediaPipe Pose Estimation.  
6. Biomechanical algorithms calculate clinically relevant gait metrics, including joint angles, cadence, stride characteristics, symmetry indices, and walking speed.  
7. The computed metrics are evaluated to identify potential gait abnormalities.  
8. OpenRouter AI generates a concise clinical interpretation based on the analytical results.  
9. A comprehensive gait analysis report is generated in PDF format.  
10. The report is securely stored and made available for future retrieval by authorized users.

Using the same workflow for each case makes the screening process easier to review and repeat.

> ![][image8]

Figure 2.3 System Operational Workflow

### 2.1.8 Site Adaptation Requirements {#2.1.8-site-adaptation-requirements}

Pedi-Growth is a cloud-based web application and does not require installation on a specific operating system.

* A reliable internet connection to support secure communication with cloud services.  
* Modern web browsers supporting HTML5, CSS3, and JavaScript.  
* HTTPS-enabled web hosting for secure data transmission.  
* Access to cloud-based backend infrastructure for AI processing and database operations.  
* Appropriate user accounts configured according to assigned roles and permissions.

No specialized sensor or wearable device is required for the current version; a clear HD video from a smartphone or digital camera is sufficient.

For more reliable results, the recording environment should meet the following conditions:

* A well-lit environment with uniform lighting.  
* Minimal visual background clutter.  
* A stable camera positioned perpendicular to the walking path.  
* Sufficient walking distance to capture multiple gait cycles.  
* Full-body visibility of the child throughout the recording.

Following these recording guidelines should improve landmark detection and make the calculated measurements more reliable.

## **2.2 Product Functions** {#2.2-product-functions}

Pedi-Growth turns an uploaded walking video into measurable gait information and presents the result through a dashboard and report.

Each user role sees only the features and patient information needed for its responsibilities.

The main functions are described in the following subsections.

### **2.2.1 User Authentication and Authorization** {#2.2.1-user-authentication-and-authorization}

Role-based access control is used so that users can access only the features and records allowed for their assigned role.

Major capabilities include:

* Secure user registration.  
* User login and logout.  
* Password management.  
* Role-based authorization.  
* Session management.  
* Protected access to patient records.

### **2.2.2 Patient Management** {#2.2.2-patient-management}

Authorized users can create, search, and update patient profiles. Each profile keeps previous gait analyses so that changes can be reviewed over time.

Major capabilities include:

* Patient registration.  
* Patient profile management.  
* Patient search.  
* Historical record retrieval.  
* Patient information updates.  
* Secure patient data storage.

### **2.2.3 Gait Video Upload** {#2.2.3-gait-video-upload}

Users can upload a gait video, preview it, and submit it for analysis. Before processing starts, the system checks the file type and basic video quality.

Major capabilities include:

* Video upload.  
* Supported file validation.  
* Video preview.  
* Upload progress tracking.  
* Recording quality verification.  
* Secure storage of uploaded videos.

### **2.2.4 AI-Based Pose Estimation** {#2.2.4-ai-based-pose-estimation}

The pose-estimation module examines the video frame by frame and identifies body landmarks without attaching physical markers to the child.

Major capabilities include:

* Automatic landmark detection.  
* Full-body skeletal tracking.  
* Multi-frame pose estimation.  
* Landmark consistency verification.  
* Missing landmark handling.  
* Coordinate extraction.

### **2.2.5 Biomechanical Gait Analysis** {#2.2.5-biomechanical-gait-analysis}

The extracted landmarks are used to calculate gait measurements and check for noticeable differences from expected walking patterns.

The calculated metrics include:

* Walking speed.  
* Cadence.  
* Step length.  
* Stride length.  
* Gait cycle duration.  
* Joint angles.  
* Limb symmetry.  
* Balance indicators.  
* Gait Pattern Severity Index (GPSI).

### **2.2.6 Gait Abnormality Screening** {#2.2.6-gait-abnormality-screening}

The calculated measurements are screened for patterns that may indicate a developmental or orthopedic concern.

Potential screening outcomes include:

* Normal gait.  
* Mild gait deviation.  
* Moderate gait deviation.  
* Severe gait deviation.  
* Recommendation for specialist consultation.

The result is only a screening outcome. A healthcare professional must review the findings before making any diagnosis or treatment decision.

### **2.2.7 AI-Generated Clinical Summary** {#2.2.7-ai-generated-clinical-summary}

The system converts the calculated values into a short, readable summary so that clinicians can quickly review the main observations.

The AI-generated summary may include:

* Overall gait assessment.  
* Significant biomechanical observations.  
* Identified asymmetries.  
* Potential clinical concerns.  
* Suggested follow-up recommendations.

### **2.2.8 Report Generation** {#2.2.8-report-generation}

After analysis, the system creates a PDF report containing patient details, calculated metrics, visual results, and the AI-assisted summary.

The report can be:

* Viewed online.  
* Downloaded as a PDF.  
* Stored within the patient's medical history.  
* Retrieved for future comparison.

### **2.2.9 Historical Analysis Management** {#2.2.9-historical-analysis-management}

Clinicians can review earlier analyses and compare them with the latest result to observe improvement, stability, or possible deterioration.

Major capabilities include:

* Viewing previous analyses.  
* Comparing historical reports.  
* Tracking gait improvement.  
* Monitoring disease progression.  
* Reviewing past AI summaries.

### **2.2.10 Administrative Functions** {#2.2.10-administrative-functions}

Administrators manage user accounts, permissions, storage, system status, and audit records. They do not take part in clinical interpretation.

Major capabilities include:

* User account management.  
* Role assignment.  
* Access control management.  
* System monitoring.  
* Report management.  
* Storage management.  
* Audit log review.  
* System configuration.

## **Figure 2.4 Use Case Diagram** {#figure-2.4-use-case-diagram}

## **2.3 User Characteristics** {#2.3-user-characteristics}

The main users of Pedi-Growth are pediatricians, community health workers, parents or caregivers, and system administrators.

Because these users have different levels of medical and technical knowledge, the interface and available functions are adjusted according to role.

## **2.3.1 Pediatricians** {#2.3.1-pediatricians}

Pediatricians are the main clinical users. They review gait measurements, AI-assisted summaries, reports, and changes in a patient's walking pattern over time.

They are expected to understand the clinical meaning of the results, but they do not need programming or computer-vision knowledge.

Responsibilities

* Register and manage patient records.  
* Upload gait videos for analysis.  
* Review biomechanical measurements.  
* Interpret AI-generated clinical summaries.  
* Download and review gait analysis reports.  
* Monitor patient progress using historical records.  
* Make informed clinical decisions based on the provided analysis.

Technical Expertise

* Basic computer literacy.  
* Familiarity with web-based healthcare applications.  
* No programming or AI knowledge required.

## **2.3.2 Community Health Workers (CHWs)** {#2.3.2-community-health-workers-(chws)}

Community Health Workers (CHWs) can use the platform for preliminary screening in community or rural settings. Their main tasks are registering patients, recording or uploading videos, and referring concerning cases to a specialist.

Responsibilities

* Register new patients.  
* Capture and upload gait videos.  
* View screening outcomes.  
* Generate preliminary reports.  
* Refer high-risk cases to pediatric specialists.

Technical Expertise

* Basic smartphone and computer operation.  
* Familiarity with patient registration procedures.  
* Minimal technical training required.

## **2.3.3 Parents and Caregivers** {#2.3.3-parents-and-caregivers}

Parents and caregivers use a simplified view to access approved reports and follow the child's previous assessments.

They may also upload a video when this has been allowed by the responsible healthcare professional.

Responsibilities

* View authorized patient reports.  
* Download gait analysis reports.  
* Review historical assessments.  
* Update personal profile information.  
* Upload videos when permitted by healthcare professionals.

Technical Expertise

* Basic web browsing skills.  
* No medical or technical knowledge required.

## **2.3.4 System Administrators** {#2.3.4-system-administrators}

System administrators are responsible for account management, permissions, system monitoring, storage, logs, and basic security configuration.

Their work is technical and operational; they do not interpret patient gait results.

Responsibilities

* Create and manage user accounts.  
* Assign user roles and permissions.  
* Monitor system performance.  
* Review audit logs.  
* Manage cloud storage resources.  
* Configure system settings.  
* Maintain overall platform security.

**Technical Expertise**

* Advanced computer literacy.  
* Knowledge of web application administration.  
* Familiarity with cloud infrastructure and database management.  
* Basic understanding of cybersecurity best practices.

## **2.3.5 User Skill Level Summary** {#2.3.5-user-skill-level-summary}

| User Role | Primary Responsibilities | Medical Knowledge | Technical Expertise |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Pediatrician | Analyze gait, review reports, monitor patients | High | Moderate |
| Community Health Worker | Register patients, upload videos, conduct screenings | Moderate | Basic |
| Parent/Caregiver | View reports, monitor progress, access patient history | Low | Basic |
| System Administrator | Manage users, monitor system, maintain security | Low | Advanced |

![][image9]

Figure 2.5 Entity Relationship Diagram

## **2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies** {#2.5-assumptions-and-dependencies}

The system depends on a stable internet connection, a modern browser, suitable recording conditions, and the availability of the selected cloud and AI services.

The main assumptions and dependencies are listed below.

## **2.5.1 Assumptions** {#2.5.1-assumptions}

The current design is based on the following assumptions:

* Users possess basic computer literacy and are capable of operating a web browser.  
* Healthcare professionals possess sufficient clinical knowledge to interpret gait analysis reports.  
* Uploaded gait videos are recorded according to the recommended recording guidelines.  
* The child remains fully visible during video capture.  
* Users provide accurate patient information during registration.  
* Internet connectivity is available during system operation.  
* Authorized users maintain the confidentiality of their login credentials.  
* Healthcare providers understand that the system functions as a screening tool rather than a diagnostic application.

## **2.5.2 Software Dependencies** {#2.5.2-software-dependencies}

Pedi-Growth depends on the following software components:

* Next.js  
* React  
* FastAPI  
* Python  
* MediaPipe  
* OpenCV  
* NumPy  
* SciPy  
* Supabase Authentication  
* PostgreSQL Database  
* Supabase Storage  
* OpenRouter AI  
* PDF Generation Libraries

Major updates, licensing changes, or compatibility problems in any of these technologies may require changes to the application.

## **2.5.3 Hardware Dependencies** {#2.5.3-hardware-dependencies}

The required hardware is limited to a normal client device, an HD-capable camera, and sufficient cloud-server resources.

* A desktop computer, laptop, tablet, or smartphone.  
* A camera capable of recording HD-quality video.  
* Sufficient server resources for AI processing.  
* Stable cloud hosting infrastructure.

## **2.5.4 Network Dependencies** {#2.5.4-network-dependencies}

An internet connection is needed for the following operations:

* User authentication.  
* Video upload.  
* Cloud storage.  
* AI summary generation.  
* Database synchronization.  
* Report retrieval.

A connection failure may pause or interrupt these tasks, but the system should avoid corrupting any data that has already been saved.

## **2.5.5 Third-Party Service Dependencies** {#2.5.5-third-party-service-dependencies}

Some parts of Pedi-Growth depend on external cloud and AI services.

These include:

* Supabase Cloud Services.  
* OpenRouter AI Services.  
* Cloud hosting infrastructure.  
* SSL certificate providers.

If one of these services is unavailable, the affected feature may stop temporarily while the remaining modules should continue to work where possible.

## **2.6 Apportioning of Requirements** {#2.6-apportioning-of-requirements}

The following features are outside the current release but are recorded as possible future improvements.

| Feature | Description | Priority |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Mobile Application | Native Android and iOS applications for remote gait screening. | High |
| Real-Time Live Analysis | Perform gait analysis directly from live camera feeds without requiring video upload. | High |
| Multi-Person Detection | Detect and analyze multiple individuals within a single video while selecting the intended patient. | Medium |
| AI Model Personalization | Improve prediction accuracy through adaptive machine learning based on historical patient data. | Medium |
| Electronic Health Record (EHR) Integration | Integrate with hospital information systems and electronic medical records. | High |
| Wearable Sensor Integration | Combine smartphone video analysis with wearable motion sensors for enhanced accuracy. | Medium |
| Telemedicine Integration | Enable clinicians to review gait analyses during virtual consultations. | Medium |
| Longitudinal Progress Analytics | Advanced dashboards for monitoring developmental trends across multiple visits. | High |
| Multilingual User Interface | Support multiple languages to improve accessibility in diverse healthcare environments. | Medium |
| Offline Data Collection | Allow temporary offline operation with automatic synchronization once internet connectivity is restored. | Medium |

These ideas came from stakeholder needs and limitations identified during project planning. They are not part of the present implementation, but the modular design should make them easier to add later.

# **3\. Specific Requirements** {#3.-specific-requirements}

This chapter lists the functional and non-functional requirements that will guide implementation and testing. Requirements use the phrase "The system shall..." whenever a clear and testable system behavior is being specified.

## **3.1 External Interface Requirements** {#3.1-external-interface-requirements}

External interfaces describe how the system interacts with users, devices, software components, and network services. Each interface category is explained below.

### 3.1.1 User Interfaces {#3.1.1-user-interfaces}

The web interface is designed for use in clinics and community screening environments, where users should be able to complete the main tasks with limited training.

The design focuses on clear navigation, simple forms, visible system feedback, and a consistent workflow from patient registration to report generation.

The main user interface components are:

* Landing Page  
* Login Page  
* User Registration Page  
* Password Recovery Page  
* User Dashboard  
* Patient Management Dashboard  
* Patient Registration Form  
* Video Upload Interface  
* Video Preview Window  
* Gait Analysis Dashboard  
* AI Clinical Summary Panel  
* Historical Analysis Page  
* PDF Report Viewer  
* User Profile Page  
* Administrator Dashboard

The interfaces should provide:

* Responsive layouts for different screen sizes.  
* Form validation with descriptive error messages.  
* Loading indicators during AI processing.  
* Success and failure notifications.  
* Accessible navigation menus.  
* Consistent color schemes and typography.  
* Role-based content visibility.

Users should be able to access the application from a modern desktop or mobile browser without installing extra software.

### **3.1.2 Hardware Interfaces** {#3.1.2-hardware-interfaces}

Pedi-Growth works with commonly available consumer hardware. The system may interact with the following devices:

| Hardware Component | Purpose |
| ----- | ----- |
| Desktop Computer | Access the web application and perform clinical analysis |
| Laptop | Portable access to the complete system |
| Tablet | Mobile access for healthcare workers |
| Smartphone Camera | Capture pediatric gait videos |
| Digital Camera | Alternative video recording device |
| Cloud Server | Execute backend services and AI processing |
| Internet Router | Provide network connectivity |

Unlike traditional gait laboratories, the system does not require:

* Infrared motion capture cameras  
* Reflective body markers  
* Force plates  
* Pressure-sensitive walkways  
* Wearable biomechanical sensors

For gait capture, the only essential device is a camera that can record a clear full-body walking sequence.

### **3.1.3 Software Interfaces** {#3.1.3-software-interfaces}

Pedi-Growth combines several frameworks, libraries, cloud platforms, and external services. Their roles are summarized below.

| Software | Role within the System |
| ----- | ----- |
| Next.js | Frontend framework |
| React | User interface rendering |
| FastAPI | Backend API development |
| Python | Backend programming language |
| MediaPipe | Human pose estimation |
| OpenCV | Image and video processing |
| NumPy | Numerical computation |
| SciPy | Mathematical and signal processing |
| OpenRouter AI | AI-generated clinical summaries |
| PostgreSQL | Relational database |
| Supabase Authentication | User authentication |
| Supabase Storage | Video and report storage |
| PDF Generation Library | Report generation |

The software components exchange data through secure REST APIs, mainly using JSON.

### **3.1.4 Communication Interfaces** {#3.1.4-communication-interfaces}

The system uses standard web technologies for secure and reliable communication. The main interfaces are:

| Protocol / Technology | Purpose |
| ----- | ----- |
| HTTPS | Secure client-server communication |
| REST API | Data exchange between frontend and backend |
| JSON | Data serialization format |
| TLS | Secure encryption during transmission |
| Supabase APIs | Authentication and database communication |
| OpenRouter API | AI-generated clinical interpretation |

Any communication containing patient, account, or authentication data shall use encrypted HTTPS connections.

## **3.2 Specific Requirements** {#3.2-specific-requirements}

The following functional requirements describe system behavior in a form that can be checked during testing. The wording "The system shall..." is kept because it makes each requirement specific and verifiable. 

### **3.2.1 Functional Requirements** {#3.2.1-functional-requirements}

### **User Authentication** {#user-authentication}

| Requirement ID | Requirement Description | Priority |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| FR-001 | The system shall allow users to register using valid personal information. | High |
| FR-002 | The system shall authenticate users using secure login credentials. | High |
| FR-003 | The system shall support secure logout functionality. | High |
| FR-004 | The system shall enforce role-based access control. | High |
| FR-005 | The system shall allow authorized users to reset forgotten passwords. | Medium |

### 

### **Patient Management** {#patient-management}

| Requirement ID | Requirement Description | Priority |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| FR-006 | The system shall allow authorized users to create patient profiles. | High |
| FR-007 | The system shall allow editing of patient information. | High |
| FR-008 | The system shall maintain historical patient records. | High |
| FR-009 | The system shall support patient search functionality. | Medium |
| FR-010 | The system shall prevent duplicate patient records where possible. | Medium |

### 

### 

### **Video Management** {#video-management}

| Requirement ID | Requirement Description | Priority |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| FR-011 | The system shall allow users to upload gait videos. | High |
| FR-012 | The system shall validate uploaded video formats before processing. | High |
| FR-013 | The system shall reject unsupported or corrupted files. | High |
| FR-014 | The system shall display upload progress indicators. | Medium |
| FR-015 | The system shall securely store uploaded videos. | High |

### 

### **AI Processing** {#ai-processing}

| Requirement ID | Requirement Description | Priority |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| FR-016 | The system shall perform markerless pose estimation using MediaPipe. | High |
| FR-017 | The system shall extract skeletal landmarks from uploaded videos. | High |
| FR-018 | The system shall calculate biomechanical gait metrics. | High |
| FR-019 | The system shall compute gait symmetry measurements. | High |
| FR-020 | The system shall estimate gait abnormality severity. | High |
| FR-021 | The system shall generate an AI-assisted clinical summary. | High |

### 

### **Report Generation** {#report-generation}

| Requirement ID | Requirement Description | Priority |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| FR-022 | The system shall generate downloadable PDF reports. | High |
| FR-023 | The system shall store generated reports within the patient's history. | High |
| FR-024 | The system shall allow users to download previous reports. | Medium |
| FR-025 | The system shall include biomechanical metrics and AI summaries within generated reports. | High |

###  **Administration** {#administration}

| Requirement ID | Requirement Description | Priority |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| FR-026 | The system shall allow administrators to manage user accounts. | High |
| FR-027 | The system shall allow administrators to assign user roles. | High |
| FR-028 | The system shall maintain audit logs of administrative actions. | Medium |
| FR-029 | The system shall monitor overall system health. | Medium |
| FR-030 | The system shall manage cloud storage resources. | Medium |

### 3.2.2 Sequence Diagrams {#3.2.2-sequence-diagrams}

The sequence diagrams show the order of communication between users, the frontend, backend, AI modules, and cloud services during important operations. They help explain how a request moves through the system from start to finish.

### 3.2.3 Classification of Specific Requirements {#3.2.3-classification-of-specific-requirements}

For easier planning and testing, the requirements are grouped according to their purpose.

**Functional Requirements**

Functional requirements describe the actions and services that users expect from the system.

These requirements include:

* User Authentication  
* Patient Registration  
* Patient Management  
* Video Upload  
* Pose Estimation  
* Biomechanical Analysis  
* Gait Abnormality Screening  
* AI Clinical Summary Generation  
* PDF Report Generation  
* Historical Record Management  
* Administrative Functions

### **Performance Requirements** {#performance-requirements}

Performance requirements define how quickly and efficiently the system should operate under normal conditions.

These include:

* Fast response times.  
* Efficient video processing.  
* Concurrent user support.  
* Scalable cloud deployment.  
* Reliable database access.

### 

### **Security Requirements** {#security-requirements}

Security requirements are included to protect patient information, user accounts, and the integrity of the application.

These include:

* Role-based authentication.  
* Secure password management.  
* HTTPS communication.  
* Encrypted data transmission.  
* Protected API endpoints.  
* Secure cloud storage.  
* Access control enforcement.  
* Audit logging.

### **Reliability Requirements** {#reliability-requirements}

Reliability requirements describe how the system should behave during normal operation and temporary failures.

These include:

* High system availability.  
* Automatic recovery from temporary failures.  
* Reliable AI processing.  
* Consistent report generation.  
* Backup and recovery mechanisms.

### **Usability Requirements** {#usability-requirements}

Usability requirements focus on making the application understandable and efficient for users with different skill levels.

These include:

* Responsive user interface.  
* Simple navigation.  
* Clear error messages.  
* Accessible forms.  
* Consistent interface design.  
* Minimal learning curve.

### 

### **Maintainability Requirements** {#maintainability-requirements}

Maintainability requirements help the development team correct problems and add future improvements without rewriting the whole system.

These include:

* Modular software architecture.  
* Well-documented APIs.  
* Reusable software components.  
* Standard coding practices.  
* Comprehensive logging.  
* Version-controlled development.

### **Scalability Requirements** {#scalability-requirements}

Scalability requirements allow the system to handle more users, records, and processing tasks in the future.

These include:

* Cloud-native deployment.  
* Horizontal backend scaling.  
* Database scalability.  
* Future AI model integration.  
* Additional healthcare module support.

## **3.3 Performance Requirements** {#3.3-performance-requirements}

The following targets describe the expected performance of Pedi-Growth, including during video processing and AI-related tasks.

| Requirement ID | Performance Requirement |
| ----- | ----- |
| PR-001 | The system shall authenticate users within **3 seconds** under normal operating conditions. |
| PR-002 | The system shall upload supported gait videos without interruption under stable network conditions. |
| PR-003 | The system shall initiate gait analysis immediately after successful upload validation. |
| PR-004 | The system shall complete gait analysis for a standard 10–20 second video within **60 seconds**, subject to server load and hardware resources. |
| PR-005 | The system shall support at least **100 concurrent authenticated users** without significant performance degradation. |
| PR-006 | The system shall generate PDF reports within **10 seconds** after completion of gait analysis. |
| PR-007 | The system shall maintain an average API response time below **2 seconds** for standard database operations. |
| PR-008 | The database shall support efficient retrieval of historical patient records with an average response time below **3 seconds**. |
| PR-009 | The application shall automatically recover from temporary network interruptions without data corruption whenever possible. |
| PR-010 | The system shall maintain high availability with a target uptime of **99.5%** or greater, excluding scheduled maintenance. |

# 

# 

## **3.4 Design Constraints** {#3.4-design-constraints}

The design and implementation of the system are limited by the following technology, architecture, browser, security, database, AI, and regulatory constraints.

### 3.4.1 Technology Constraints {#3.4.1-technology-constraints}

The current project uses the technology stack shown below. Changing a major component may affect compatibility and require additional testing.

| Component | Selected Technology |
| ----- | ----- |
| Frontend | Next.js with React |
| Backend | FastAPI (Python) |
| Programming Language | Python, JavaScript, TypeScript |
| Database | PostgreSQL (Supabase) |
| Authentication | Supabase Authentication |
| Cloud Storage | Supabase Storage |
| Computer Vision | OpenCV |
| Pose Estimation | MediaPipe |
| AI Summary Generation | OpenRouter AI |
| Version Control | Git and GitHub |

Therefore, replacing one of the selected technologies should be treated as a major design decision rather than a minor implementation change.

### 3.4.2 Architectural Constraints {#3.4.2-architectural-constraints}

A layered architecture is used to keep the interface, application logic, AI processing, and data storage separate.

The architecture shall consist of:

* Presentation Layer  
* Application Layer  
* AI Processing Layer  
* Data Layer

Each module shall have a clear responsibility and communicate with other modules through defined REST APIs.

Business logic shall remain outside the presentation layer so that interface changes do not directly affect core processing.

### 3.4.3 Browser Compatibility Constraints {#3.4.3-browser-compatibility-constraints}

The application shall support current standards-compliant web browsers.

Supported browsers include:

* Google Chrome  
* Microsoft Edge  
* Mozilla Firefox  
* Safari

Older browser versions that no longer receive official support are outside the project scope.

### 3.4.4 Security Constraints {#3.4.4-security-constraints}

Because the system handles pediatric health information, the following security controls are required:

* HTTPS-only communication.  
* Role-based authorization.  
* Secure password storage.  
* Encrypted authentication tokens.  
* Input validation.  
* Output sanitization.  
* Protection against SQL injection.  
* Protection against Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).  
* Protection against Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF).  
* Secure API authentication.  
* Audit logging of critical administrative actions.

### 3.4.5 Database Constraints {#3.4.5-database-constraints}

Patient and system records shall be stored in a relational PostgreSQL database.

Database constraints include:

* Unique user identifiers.  
* Unique patient identifiers.  
* Referential integrity between related tables.  
* Transaction consistency.  
* Automatic timestamp generation.  
* Secure access permissions.

The schema should allow new modules and data fields to be added later without major restructuring.

### 3.4.6 AI Model Constraints {#3.4.6-ai-model-constraints}

The AI pipeline depends on computer-vision libraries and an external language-model service, so the system must handle incomplete or unavailable AI output carefully.

The system shall:

* Validate extracted pose landmarks before analysis.  
* Handle missing landmark information gracefully.  
* Continue processing whenever partial landmark recovery is possible.  
* Clearly distinguish AI-generated summaries from clinical diagnoses.  
* Display confidence information where applicable.

Any AI-generated output shall be clearly presented as supporting information, not as a confirmed medical conclusion.

### 3.4.7 Regulatory Constraints {#3.4.7-regulatory-constraints}

Pedi-Growth processes health-related information, so any real deployment must follow the privacy laws and institutional data policies that apply in its location.

The system should be designed so that it can later be adapted to requirements such as:

* HIPAA (United States)  
* GDPR (European Union)  
* Local healthcare data protection regulations

Key compliance concerns include secure storage, access control, encrypted communication, consent, and responsible use of patient data.

## **3.5 Software System Attributes** {#3.5-software-system-attributes}

This section describes the quality characteristics expected from the system in addition to its functional features.

### **3.5.1 Reliability** {#3.5.1-reliability}

For this project, reliability means that Pedi-Growth should produce consistent results, protect saved records, and respond safely when a component fails.

To ensure system reliability:

* The system shall validate all uploaded data before processing.  
* AI processing failures shall be handled gracefully.  
* Database transactions shall preserve data consistency.  
* Temporary failures shall not corrupt patient records.  
* Unexpected exceptions shall be logged for future analysis.  
* System components shall recover from transient failures whenever possible.

Overall, the application should remain dependable during repeated clinical or community screening use.

### **3.5.2 Availability** {#3.5.2-availability}

Pedi-Growth should remain available to authorized users as much as possible, apart from planned maintenance or unavoidable service outages.

Availability objectives include:

* Cloud-based deployment for continuous accessibility.  
* High availability database services.  
* Automatic service recovery after temporary failures.  
* Scheduled maintenance during low-usage periods.  
* Reliable backup and restoration mechanisms.

The target system availability is **99.5%** or higher, excluding planned maintenance.

### **3.5.3 Security** {#3.5.3-security}

Security is especially important because the platform stores pediatric health information and user account data.

The system shall provide:

* Secure authentication.  
* Role-based authorization.  
* Encrypted communication.  
* Protected API endpoints.  
* Secure password hashing.  
* Session management.  
* Secure file uploads.  
* Audit logging.  
* Input validation.  
* Protection against common web vulnerabilities.

A user shall only be able to access patient information that is permitted by the user's assigned role.

### **3.5.4 Maintainability** {#3.5.4-maintainability}

The codebase should be organized so that the system can be corrected, updated, and extended after the first release.

Maintainability shall be achieved through:

* Modular architecture.  
* Well-documented source code.  
* Standard coding conventions.  
* API documentation.  
* Separation of concerns.  
* Version control.  
* Reusable software components.  
* Automated testing where applicable.

Following these practices should reduce the effort required for future fixes and feature updates.

### **3.5.5 Usability** {#3.5.5-usability}

The system should be understandable for both technical users and people with only basic computer skills.

Usability objectives include:

* Simple navigation.  
* Responsive layouts.  
* Consistent interface design.  
* Clear instructions.  
* Informative error messages.  
* Minimal learning curve.  
* Accessibility for users with varying technical experience.

A healthcare professional should be able to complete the basic workflow after a short introduction to the system.

### **3.5.6 Scalability** {#3.5.6-scalability}

The architecture should support future growth without requiring the complete system to be redesigned.

The system should accommodate:

* Increasing numbers of users.  
* Growing patient databases.  
* Additional healthcare facilities.  
* More advanced AI models.  
* Additional disease screening modules.  
* Integration with hospital information systems.  
* Increased computational workloads.

Because the system is modular, the frontend, backend, AI processing, and database resources can be expanded separately when needed.

## **3.6 Other Requirements** {#3.6-other-requirements}

The following additional requirements cover logging, error handling, future integration, and ethical use of the system.

### **3.6.1 Logging and Audit Trails** {#3.6.1-logging-and-audit-trails}

* The application shall record important system activities in logs.  
* The following events will be logged:  
* User authentication.  
* Patient record modifications.  
* Video uploads.  
* Report generation.  
* Administrative actions.  
* System errors.  
* Security-related events.  
* Only authorized administrators shall have access to audit logs.

### 3.6.2 Error Handling {#3.6.2-error-handling}

* When an error occurs, the system shall explain the problem in clear language instead of failing without feedback.  
* Error handling shall include:  
* Human-readable error messages.  
* Validation feedback.  
* Recovery suggestions (where applicable).  
* Exception logging.  
* Graceful failure (without sudden termination of the application).

### 3.6.3 Future Integration Support {#3.6.3-future-integration-support}

* The architecture should allow future integration with healthcare systems and new technologies through documented interfaces, without tightly binding the current application to one external platform.  
* Potential integrations include:  
* Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems.  
* Hospital Management Systems.  
* Telemedicine platforms.  
* Wearable motion sensors.  
* Cloud AI services.  
* National healthcare databases.

### 3.6.4 Ethical Considerations {#3.6.4-ethical-considerations}

* Pedi-Growth is intended to support screening and clinical review. It is not designed to diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent a disease on its own.  
* Accordingly:  
* AI-generated results shall be presented as observations or recommendations, not as diagnoses.  
* All clinical decisions shall be the responsibility of healthcare professionals.  
* Users shall be informed that the application is a screening and decision-support tool.  
* Patient privacy and confidentiality shall remain a major design priority.  
* The system shall show the measurable gait values used in the report instead of presenting only an AI-generated interpretation.

# **4\. Supporting Information** {#4.-supporting-information}

This chapter contains supporting material that helps explain how the requirements were collected, prioritized, and reviewed. It also records stakeholder interests, project risks, future ideas, and important terms that are not explained in detail in the main requirements sections.

## **4.1 Appendices** {#4.1-appendices}

The appendices summarize information collected during project planning, research, requirement analysis, and design. They provide background for the decisions recorded in this SRS:

* **High Awareness & Support:** Participants recognized the need for improved pediatric gait screening and strongly support an AI-based smartphone solution.  
* **Lack of Early Screening:** Many had observed atypical gait patterns in children but did not seek help, highlighting the need for more accessible early screening tools.  
* **Access Barriers:** Limited access to specialized gait laboratories and equipment was cited as a primary barrier to obtaining referrals.  
* **Desired Features:** Strong interest exists for a free, user-friendly app capable of performing gait assessments within two minutes.  
* **Reliability Requirements:** Participants expect the AI screening tool to have at least 80% reliability (ICC \> 0.80).  
* **Market Need:** Many noted a need for 1-4 gait analyses per week and expressed difficulty accessing specialized centers.  
* **Preferred Metrics:** Joint angles, symmetry, and range of motion were identified as the most useful biomechanical measurements.  
* **Community Impact:** Participants believe an open-source smartphone platform could revolutionize community healthcare by enabling early detection, streamlining referral pathways, and improving access for underserved areas.

### 

### 

### 

### 

### **Appendix A \- Requirement Elicitation** {#appendix-a---requirement-elicitation}

| Requirement Elicitation Method/Source | Description |
| ----- | ----- |
| Literature Review | Analysis of academic publications on pediatric gait analysis, biomechanics, and markerless human pose estimation research. |
| Stakeholder Analysis | Interviews and discussions with pediatricians, health workers, and caregivers to understand clinical and user needs. |
| Clinical Workflow Evaluation | Mapping existing screening procedures to identify automation opportunities for biomechanical metric extraction. |
| Project Supervision | Regular consultations with the academic supervisor to ensure technical feasibility and software engineering compliance. |

After collection, the requirements were grouped into functional, non-functional, security, performance, and future-development categories.

## 

## 

## 

### **Appendix B \- Stakeholder Analysis** {#appendix-b---stakeholder-analysis}

| Stakeholder | Role | Primary Interest |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Pediatricians | Clinical Users | Accurate gait analysis and reliable clinical reports |
| Community Health Workers | Primary Healthcare Providers | Easy-to-use screening tools for community healthcare |
| Parents/Caregivers | End Users | Monitoring child development and accessing reports |
| System Administrator | Technical Administrator | Secure operation and system maintenance |
| Development Team | Software Engineers | Successful implementation and maintenance |
| Academic Supervisor | Project Supervisor | Project quality and academic compliance |

This stakeholder review helped the team decide which functions were essential for the first version and which could be postponed.

### **Appendix C \- Requirement Prioritization (MoSCoW Analysis)** {#appendix-c---requirement-prioritization-(moscow-analysis)}

The MoSCoW method was used to separate essential requirements from useful but non-essential features.

| Priority | Description | Examples |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Must Have | Essential features required for system operation. | Authentication, Patient Management, Video Upload, AI Analysis |
| Should Have | Important features that improve usability but are not critical for initial deployment. | Historical Reports, AI Clinical Summary, Administrative Dashboard |
| Could Have | Additional features that enhance user experience if resources permit. | Progress Analytics, Advanced Visualizations, Telemedicine Integration |
| Won't Have (Current Release) | Planned for future versions but excluded from the current scope. | Mobile Application, Wearable Sensor Integration, Offline Mode |

This approach keeps the first release focused on the core screening workflow while leaving room for later improvements.

### **Appendix D \- Risk Analysis** {#appendix-d---risk-analysis}

The main risks identified during planning and their proposed mitigation steps are listed below.

| Risk | Potential Impact | Mitigation Strategy |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Poor-quality gait videos | Reduced analysis accuracy | Provide recording guidelines and perform video quality validation |
| AI model limitations | Incorrect or incomplete interpretation | Present AI outputs as decision-support rather than diagnosis |
| Internet connectivity issues | Interrupted uploads or report retrieval | Implement retry mechanisms and robust error handling |
| Third-party service outages | Temporary loss of AI summary generation | Continue core gait analysis while notifying users of unavailable services |
| Unauthorized system access | Data privacy breach | Enforce role-based authentication, encryption, and audit logging |
| Cloud infrastructure failure | Temporary service interruption | Utilize managed cloud services with backup and recovery procedures |

Regular testing, monitoring, backups, and maintenance should reduce these risks during development and operation.

### Appendix E \- Future Scope {#appendix-e---future-scope}

The current project focuses on video-based pediatric gait screening, but several useful extensions have been identified for later versions.

Possible future developments include:

| Feature | Description |
| ----- | ----- |
| Native Mobile Apps | Dedicated Android and iOS applications for improved recording and remote access. |
| Real-time Gait Analysis | Processing gait metrics directly from live camera streams without manual upload. |
| EHR Integration | Seamless synchronization with standard Electronic Health Record systems. |
| Wearable Sensor Support | Integration with IMU sensors for multi-modal biomechanical data capture. |
| Advanced AI Models | Personalized machine learning models trained on longitudinal patient data. |
| Telemedicine Features | In-app video consultations and collaborative report reviewing. |
| Progress Visualization | Interactive trend charts for monitoring gait development over years. |
| Multilingual Interface | Localization for diverse healthcare environments and non-English regions. |
| Offline Data Collection | Local recording and caching with cloud sync for resource-constrained areas. |
| Predictive Analytics | Advanced screening based on developmental trend forecasting. |

### **Appendix F \- Glossary** {#appendix-f---glossary}

| Term | Definition |
| ----- | ----- |
| Gait Analysis | The study of human walking patterns to evaluate movement characteristics and identify abnormalities. |
| Pose Estimation | A computer-vision method used to locate and track human body landmarks in an image or video. |
| Markerless Motion Capture | A method of recording and analyzing movement without attaching physical markers or sensors to the person. |
| Biomechanics | The scientific study of forces and movement in the human body. |
| Clinical Decision Support | Technology that provides organized information to help healthcare professionals make informed decisions. |
| AI Clinical Summary | A natural-language summary generated from the biomechanical measurements calculated by the system. |
| GPSI | Gait Pattern Severity Index; a combined score used by the system to summarize the estimated severity of detected gait deviations. |
| REST API | A web-service approach that allows software components to communicate through standard HTTP requests. |
| Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) | An access-control method in which permissions are assigned according to a user's role. |
| Screening Tool | A tool used to identify people who may need further clinical assessment; it does not provide a final diagnosis. |

# **Conclusion** {#conclusion}

This SRS presents the planned structure of Pedi-Growth, including its clinical screening workflow, technical components, user roles, and software requirements The requirements provide a practical guide for designing, implementing, testing, and improving the platform. They also make the project scope clearer and provide a basis for checking whether the completed system meets its intended goals.
